title: Heresy
excerpt: One of the most surprising things I've witnessed in my lifetime is the rebirth of the concept of heresy.
content_html:
April 2022

One of the most surprising things I've witnessed in my lifetime is the rebirth of the concept of heresy.

In his excellent biography of Newton, Richard Westfall writes about the moment when he was elected a fellow of Trinity College:

Supported comfortably, Newton was free to devote himself wholly to whatever he chose. To remain on, he had only to avoid the three unforgivable sins: crime, heresy, and marriage. [1]
The first time I read that, in the 1990s, it sounded amusingly medieval. How strange, to have to avoid committing heresy. But when I reread it 20 years later it sounded like a description of contemporary employment.

There are an ever-increasing number of opinions you can be fired for. Those doing the firing don't use the word "heresy" to describe them, but structurally they're equivalent. Structurally there are two distinctive things about heresy: (1) that it takes priority over the question of truth or falsity, and (2) that it outweighs everything else the speaker has done.

For example, when someone calls a statement "x-ist," they're also implicitly saying that this is the end of the discussion. They do not, having said this, go on to consider whether the statement is true or not. Using such labels is the conversational equivalent of signalling an exception. That's one of the reasons they're used: to end a discussion.

If you find yourself talking to someone who uses these labels a lot, it might be worthwhile to ask them explicitly if they believe any babies are being thrown out with the bathwater. Can a statement be x-ist, for whatever value of x, and also true? If the answer is yes, then they're admitting to banning the truth. That's obvious enough that I'd guess most would answer no. But if they answer no, it's easy to show that they're mistaken, and that in practice such labels are applied to statements regardless of their truth or falsity.

The clearest evidence of this is that whether a statement is considered x-ist often depends on who said it. Truth doesn't work that way. The same statement can't be true when one person says it, but x-ist, and therefore false, when another person does.

[2]

The other distinctive thing about heresies, compared to ordinary opinions, is that the public expression of them outweighs everything else the speaker has done. In ordinary matters, like knowledge of history, or taste in music, you're judged by the average of your opinions. A heresy is qualitatively different. It's like dropping a chunk of uranium onto the scale.

Back in the day (and still, in some places) the punishment for heresy was death. You could have led a life of exemplary goodness, but if you publicly doubted, say, the divinity of Christ, you were going to burn. Nowadays, in civilized countries, heretics only get fired in the metaphorical sense, by losing their jobs. But the structure of the situation is the same: the heresy outweighs everything else. You could have spent the last ten years saving children's lives, but if you express certain opinions, you're automatically fired.

It's much the same as if you committed a crime. No matter how virtuously you've lived, if you commit a crime, you must still suffer the penalty of the law. Having lived a previously blameless life might mitigate the punishment, but it doesn't affect whether you're guilty or not.

A heresy is an opinion whose expression is treated like a crime — one that makes some people feel not merely that you're mistaken, but that you should be punished. Indeed, their desire to see you punished is often stronger than it would be if you'd committed an actual crime. There are many on the far left who believe strongly in the reintegration of felons (as I do myself), and yet seem to feel that anyone guilty of certain heresies should never work again.

There are always some heresies — some opinions you'd be punished for expressing. But there are a lot more now than there were a few decades ago, and even those who are happy about this would have to agree that it's so.

Why? Why has this antiquated-sounding religious concept come back in a secular form? And why now?

You need two ingredients for a wave of intolerance: intolerant people, and an ideology to guide them. The intolerant people are always there. They exist in every sufficiently large society. That's why waves of intolerance can arise so suddenly; all they need is something to set them off.

I've already written an

essay describing the aggressively conventional-minded. The short version is that people can be classified in two dimensions according to (1) how independent- or conventional-minded they are, and (2) how aggressive they are about it. The aggressively conventional-minded are the enforcers of orthodoxy.

Normally they're only locally visible. They're the grumpy, censorious people in a group — the ones who are always first to complain when something violates the current rules of propriety. But occasionally, like a vector field whose elements become aligned, a large number of aggressively conventional-minded people unite behind some ideology all at once. Then they become much more of a problem, because a mob dynamic takes over, where the enthusiasm of each participant is increased by the enthusiasm of the others.

The most notorious 20th century case may have been the Cultural Revolution. Though initiated by Mao to undermine his rivals, the Cultural Revolution was otherwise mostly a grass-roots phenomenon. Mao said in essence: There are heretics among us. Seek them out and punish them. And that's all the aggressively conventional-minded ever need to hear. They went at it with the delight of dogs chasing squirrels.

To unite the conventional-minded, an ideology must have many of the features of a religion. In particular it must have strict and arbitrary rules that adherents can demonstrate their

purity by obeying, and its adherents must believe that anyone who obeys these rules is ipso facto morally superior to anyone who doesn't. [3]

In the late 1980s a new ideology of this type appeared in US universities. It had a very strong component of moral purity, and the aggressively conventional-minded seized upon it with their usual eagerness — all the more because the relaxation of social norms in the preceding decades meant there had been less and less to forbid. The resulting wave of intolerance has been eerily similar in form to the Cultural Revolution, though fortunately much smaller in magnitude.

[4]

I've deliberately avoided mentioning any specific heresies here. Partly because one of the universal tactics of heretic hunters, now as in the past, is to accuse those who disapprove of the way in which they suppress ideas of being heretics themselves. Indeed, this tactic is so consistent that you could use it as a way of detecting witch hunts in any era.

And that's the second reason I've avoided mentioning any specific heresies. I want this essay to work in the future, not just now. And unfortunately it probably will. The aggressively conventional-minded will always be among us, looking for things to forbid. All they need is an ideology to tell them what. And it's unlikely the current one will be the last.

There are aggressively conventional-minded people on both the right and the left. The reason the current wave of intolerance comes from the left is simply because the new unifying ideology happened to come from the left. The next one might come from the right. Imagine what that would be like.

Fortunately in western countries the suppression of heresies is nothing like as bad as it used to be. Though the window of opinions you can express publicly has narrowed in the last decade, it's still much wider than it was a few hundred years ago. The problem is the derivative. Up till about 1985 the window had been growing ever wider. Anyone looking into the future in 1985 would have expected freedom of expression to continue to increase. Instead it has decreased.

[5]

The situation is similar to what's happened with infectious diseases like measles. Anyone looking into the future in 2010 would have expected the number of measles cases in the US to continue to decrease. Instead, thanks to anti-vaxxers, it has increased. The absolute number is still not that high. The problem is the derivative.

[6]

In both cases it's hard to know how much to worry. Is it really dangerous to society as a whole if a handful of extremists refuse to get their kids vaccinated, or shout down speakers at universities? The point to start worrying is presumably when their efforts start to spill over into everyone else's lives. And in both cases that does seem to be happening.

So it's probably worth spending some amount of effort on pushing back to keep open the window of free expression. My hope is that this essay will help form social antibodies not just against current efforts to suppress ideas, but against the concept of heresy in general. That's the real prize. How do you disable the concept of heresy? Since the Enlightenment, western societies have discovered many techniques for doing that, but there are surely more to be discovered.

Overall I'm optimistic. Though the trend in freedom of expression has been bad over the last decade, it's been good over the longer term. And there are signs that the current wave of intolerance is peaking. Independent-minded people I talk to seem more confident than they did a few years ago. On the other side, even some of the

leaders are starting to wonder if things have gone too far. And popular culture among the young has already moved on. All we have to do is keep pushing back, and the wave collapses. And then we'll be net ahead, because as well as having defeated this wave, we'll also have developed new tactics for resisting the next one.

Notes

[

1] Or more accurately, biographies of Newton, since Westfall wrote two: a long version called Never at Rest, and a shorter one called The Life of Isaac Newton. Both are great. The short version moves faster, but the long one is full of interesting and often very funny details. This passage is the same in both.

[

2] Another more subtle but equally damning bit of evidence is that claims of x-ism are never qualified. You never hear anyone say that a statement is "probably x-ist" or "almost certainly y-ist." If claims of x-ism were actually claims about truth, you'd expect to see "probably" in front of "x-ist" as often as you see it in front of "fallacious."

[

3] The rules must be strict, but they need not be demanding. So the most effective type of rules are those about superficial matters, like doctrinal minutiae, or the precise words adherents must use. Such rules can be made extremely complicated, and yet don't repel potential converts by requiring significant sacrifice.

The superficial demands of orthodoxy make it an inexpensive substitute for virtue. And that in turn is one of the reasons orthodoxy is so attractive to bad people. You could be a horrible person, and yet as long as you're orthodox, you're better than everyone who isn't.

[

4] Arguably there were two. The first had died down somewhat by 2000, but was followed by a second in the 2010s, probably caused by social media.

[

5] Fortunately most of those trying to suppress ideas today still respect Enlightenment principles enough to pay lip service to them. They know they're not supposed to ban ideas per se, so they have to recast the ideas as causing "harm," which sounds like something that can be banned. The more extreme try to claim speech itself is violence, or even that silence is. But strange as it may sound, such gymnastics are a good sign. We'll know we're really in trouble when they stop bothering to invent pretenses for banning ideas — when, like the medieval church, they say "Damn right we're banning ideas, and in fact here's a list of them."

[

6] People only have the luxury of ignoring the medical consensus about vaccines because vaccines have worked so well. If we didn't have any vaccines at all, the mortality rate would be so high that most current anti-vaxxers would be begging for them. And the situation with freedom of expression is similar. It's only because they live in a world created by the Enlightenment that kids from the suburbs can play at banning ideas.Thanks to Marc Andreessen, Chris Best, Trevor Blackwell, Nicholas Christakis, Daniel Gackle, Jonathan Haidt, Claire Lehmann, Jessica Livingston, Greg Lukianoff, Robert Morris, and Garry Tan for reading drafts of this.

4] Arguably there were two. The first had died down somewhat by 2000, but was followed by a second in the 2010s, probably caused by social media.

[

5] Fortunately most of those trying to suppress ideas today still respect Enlightenment principles enough to pay lip service to them. They know they're not supposed to ban ideas per se, so they have to recast the ideas as causing "harm," which sounds like something that can be banned. The more extreme try to claim speech itself is violence, or even that silence is. But strange as it may sound, such gymnastics are a good sign. We'll know we're really in trouble when they stop bothering to invent pretenses for banning ideas — when, like the medieval church, they say "Damn right we're banning ideas, and in fact here's a list of them."

[

6] People only have the luxury of ignoring the medical consensus about vaccines because vaccines have worked so well. If we didn't have any vaccines at all, the mortality rate would be so high that most current anti-vaxxers would be begging for them. And the situation with freedom of expression is similar. It's only because they live in a world created by the Enlightenment that kids from the suburbs can play at banning ideas.Thanks to Marc Andreessen, Chris Best, Trevor Blackwell, Nicholas Christakis, Daniel Gackle, Jonathan Haidt, Claire Lehmann, Jessica Livingston, Greg Lukianoff, Robert Morris, and Garry Tan for reading drafts of this.

sentences.en: April 2022One of the most surprising things I've witnessed in my lifetime is the rebirth of the concept of heresy.In his excellent biography of Newton, Richard Westfall writes about the moment when he was elected a fellow of Trinity College: Supported comfortably, Newton was free to devote himself wholly to whatever he chose.
sentences.zh: 四月 2022我一生中目睹的最令人惊讶的事情之一就是异端概念的重生。理查德·韦斯特弗尔（Richard Westfall）在他出色的牛顿传记中写道，他被选为三一学院院士的那一刻：在舒适的支持下，牛顿可以自由地完全奉献自己所选择的任何事情。

sentences.en: To remain on, he had only to avoid the three unforgivable sins: crime, heresy, and marriage. [1] The first time I read that, in the 1990s, it sounded amusingly medieval.
sentences.zh: 为了继续留下来，他只需要避免三宗不可饶恕的罪孽：犯罪、异端和婚姻。 [1] 我第一次读到它是在 20 世纪 90 年代，它听起来很有趣的中世纪。

sentences.en: How strange, to have to avoid committing heresy.
sentences.zh: 多么奇怪啊，必须避免犯异端邪说。

sentences.en: But when I reread it 20 years later it sounded like a description of contemporary employment.There are an ever-increasing number of opinions you can be fired for.
sentences.zh: 但 20 年后我重读它时，它听起来像是对当代就业的描述。有越来越多的观点可以让你被解雇。

sentences.en: Those doing the firing don't use the word "heresy" to describe them, but structurally they're equivalent.
sentences.zh: 那些进行解雇的人并不使用“异端”这个词来描述他们，但在结构上他们是等效的。

sentences.en: Structurally there are two distinctive things about heresy: (1) that it takes priority over the question of truth or falsity, and (2) that it outweighs everything else the speaker has done.For example, when someone calls a statement "x-ist," they're also implicitly saying that this is the end of the discussion.
sentences.zh: 从结构上讲，异端有两个独特之处：（1）它优先于真假问题，（2）它比演讲者所做的其他一切都重要。例如，当有人称某个陈述为“x-ist”时，他们也含蓄地表示讨论到此结束。

sentences.en: They do not, having said this, go on to consider whether the statement is true or not.
sentences.zh: 说了这句话后，他们并没有继续考虑这个说法是否属实。

sentences.en: Using such labels is the conversational equivalent of signalling an exception.
sentences.zh: 使用此类标签相当于会话中发出异常信号。

sentences.en: That's one of the reasons they're used: to end a discussion.If you find yourself talking to someone who uses these labels a lot, it might be worthwhile to ask them explicitly if they believe any babies are being thrown out with the bathwater.
sentences.zh: 这就是使用它们的原因之一：结束讨论。如果您发现自己正在与经常使用这些标签的人交谈，那么可能值得明确询问他们是否认为任何婴儿都被与洗澡水一起倒掉了。

sentences.en: Can a statement be x-ist, for whatever value of x, and also true?
sentences.zh: 无论 x 的值如何，一个陈述是否可以是 x-ist 并且为真？

sentences.en: If the answer is yes, then they're admitting to banning the truth.
sentences.zh: 如果答案是肯定的，那么他们就承认禁止真相。

sentences.en: That's obvious enough that I'd guess most would answer no.
sentences.zh: 这很明显，我猜大多数人都会回答“不”。

sentences.en: But if they answer no, it's easy to show that they're mistaken, and that in practice such labels are applied to statements regardless of their truth or falsity.The clearest evidence of this is that whether a statement is considered x-ist often depends on who said it.
sentences.zh: 但如果他们的回答是否定的，那么很容易表明他们是错误的，而且在实践中，这样的标签会被应用到声明上，无论其真实性或虚假性。最明显的证据是，一个声明是否被认为是x-ist通常取决于谁说它。

sentences.en: Truth doesn't work that way.
sentences.zh: 真相不是这样的。

sentences.en: The same statement can't be true when one person says it, but x-ist, and therefore false, when another person does. [2]The other distinctive thing about heresies, compared to ordinary opinions, is that the public expression of them outweighs everything else the speaker has done.
sentences.zh: 同样的陈述当一个人说出来时不可能是真的，但当另一个人这么说时就不是了，因此是错误的。 [2]与普通观点相比，异端邪说的另一个独特之处在于，异端邪说的公开表达超过了演讲者所做的一切。

sentences.en: In ordinary matters, like knowledge of history, or taste in music, you're judged by the average of your opinions.
sentences.zh: 在平常的事情上，比如历史知识或音乐品味，人们会根据你的平均意见来评价你。

sentences.en: A heresy is qualitatively different.
sentences.zh: 异端在本质上是不同的。

sentences.en: It's like dropping a chunk of uranium onto the scale.Back in the day (and still, in some places) the punishment for heresy was death.
sentences.zh: 这就像把一大块铀扔到天平上。在过去（现在仍然在某些地方），对异端的惩罚是死刑。

sentences.en: You could have led a life of exemplary goodness, but if you publicly doubted, say, the divinity of Christ, you were going to burn.
sentences.zh: 你本来可以过着模范善良的生活，但如果你公开怀疑，比如说，基督的神性，你就会被烧死。

sentences.en: Nowadays, in civilized countries, heretics only get fired in the metaphorical sense, by losing their jobs.
sentences.zh: 如今，在文明国家，异教徒只会在隐喻意义上被解雇，即失去工作。

sentences.en: But the structure of the situation is the same: the heresy outweighs everything else.
sentences.zh: 但情况的结构是一样的：异端压倒一切。

sentences.en: You could have spent the last ten years saving children's lives, but if you express certain opinions, you're automatically fired.It's much the same as if you committed a crime.
sentences.zh: 你本可以在过去的十年里拯救孩子们的生命，但如果你表达某些观点，你就会自动被解雇。这就像你犯了罪一样。

sentences.en: No matter how virtuously you've lived, if you commit a crime, you must still suffer the penalty of the law.
sentences.zh: 无论你的生活多么有道德，如果你犯了罪，你仍然必须受到法律的惩罚。

sentences.en: Having lived a previously blameless life might mitigate the punishment, but it doesn't affect whether you're guilty or not.A heresy is an opinion whose expression is treated like a crime — one that makes some people feel not merely that you're mistaken, but that you should be punished.
sentences.zh: 过着无可指责的生活可能会减轻惩罚，但这并不影响你是否有罪。异端邪说是一种观点，其表达被视为犯罪——这种观点让一些人不仅觉得你错了，而且觉得你应该受到惩罚。

sentences.en: Indeed, their desire to see you punished is often stronger than it would be if you'd committed an actual crime.
sentences.zh: 事实上，他们希望看到你受到惩罚的愿望往往比你真正犯罪时更强烈。

sentences.en: There are many on the far left who believe strongly in the reintegration of felons (as I do myself), and yet seem to feel that anyone guilty of certain heresies should never work again.There are always some heresies — some opinions you'd be punished for expressing.
sentences.zh: 有许多极左人士坚信重罪犯重新融入社会（就像我自己一样），但似乎觉得任何犯有某些异端邪说的人都不应该再工作。总有一些异端邪说——有些观点你会因为表达而受到惩罚。

sentences.en: But there are a lot more now than there were a few decades ago, and even those who are happy about this would have to agree that it's so.Why?
sentences.zh: 但现在比几十年前多了很多，即使是那些对此感到高兴的人也不得不同意事实如此。为什么呢？

sentences.en: Why has this antiquated-sounding religious concept come back in a secular form?
sentences.zh: 为什么这个听起来过时的宗教概念又以世俗的形式回归呢？

sentences.en: And why now?You need two ingredients for a wave of intolerance: intolerant people, and an ideology to guide them.
sentences.zh: 为什么是现在？引发不宽容浪潮需要两个要素：不宽容的人以及引导他们的意识形态。

sentences.en: The intolerant people are always there.
sentences.zh: 不宽容的人总是存在的。

sentences.en: They exist in every sufficiently large society.
sentences.zh: 它们存在于每一个足够大的社会中。

sentences.en: That's why waves of intolerance can arise so suddenly; all they need is something to set them off.I've already written an essay describing the aggressively conventional-minded.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么不宽容的浪潮会突然出现。他们所需要的只是一些东西来引爆他们。我已经写过一篇文章来描述激进的传统思想。

sentences.en: The short version is that people can be classified in two dimensions according to (1) how independent- or conventional-minded they are, and (2) how aggressive they are about it.
sentences.zh: 简而言之，人们可以根据（1）他们的独立性或传统思想程度，以及（2）他们对此的激进程度进行两个维度的分类。

sentences.en: The aggressively conventional-minded are the enforcers of orthodoxy.Normally they're only locally visible.
sentences.zh: 思想激进、传统观念强的人是正统观念的执行者。通常他们只在当地可见。

sentences.en: They're the grumpy, censorious people in a group — the ones who are always first to complain when something violates the current rules of propriety.
sentences.zh: 他们是群体中脾气暴躁、挑剔的人——当有事情违反现行礼仪规则时，他们总是第一个抱怨的人。

sentences.en: But occasionally, like a vector field whose elements become aligned, a large number of aggressively conventional-minded people unite behind some ideology all at once.
sentences.zh: 但偶尔，就像元素对齐的向量场一样，大量具有攻击性的传统思想的人会同时团结在某种意识形态的支持下。

sentences.en: Then they become much more of a problem, because a mob dynamic takes over, where the enthusiasm of each participant is increased by the enthusiasm of the others.The most notorious 20th century case may have been the Cultural Revolution.
sentences.zh: 然后它们就成为一个更大的问题，因为暴民动态占据了主导地位，每个参与者的热情都因其他参与者的热情而增加。20世纪最臭名昭著的案例可能是文化大革命。

sentences.en: Though initiated by Mao to undermine his rivals, the Cultural Revolution was otherwise mostly a grass-roots phenomenon.
sentences.zh: 尽管文化大革命是毛泽东发起的，目的是削弱他的对手，但除此之外，文化大革命主要是一种草根现象。

sentences.en: Mao said in essence: There are heretics among us.
sentences.zh: 毛泽东实质上说：我们中间有异端。

sentences.en: Seek them out and punish them.
sentences.zh: 找出他们并惩罚他们。

sentences.en: And that's all the aggressively conventional-minded ever need to hear.
sentences.zh: 这就是那些思想激进、传统观念强的人所需要听到的。

sentences.en: They went at it with the delight of dogs chasing squirrels.To unite the conventional-minded, an ideology must have many of the features of a religion.
sentences.zh: 他们像狗追松鼠一样高兴。 为了团结传统思想，意识形态必须具有宗教的许多特征。

sentences.en: In particular it must have strict and arbitrary rules that adherents can demonstrate their purity by obeying, and its adherents must believe that anyone who obeys these rules is ipso facto morally superior to anyone who doesn't. [3]In the late 1980s a new ideology of this type appeared in US universities.
sentences.zh: 特别是，它必须有严格和任意的规则，追随者可以通过遵守来证明自己的纯洁性，并且它的追随者必须相信，任何遵守这些规则的人在道德上事实上都优于任何不遵守这些规则的人。 [3]20世纪80年代末，美国大学中出现了这种类型的新意识形态。

sentences.en: It had a very strong component of moral purity, and the aggressively conventional-minded seized upon it with their usual eagerness — all the more because the relaxation of social norms in the preceding decades meant there had been less and less to forbid.
sentences.zh: 它具有很强的道德纯洁性成分，而那些具有激进传统思想的人以他们一贯的渴望抓住了它——更重要的是，因为过去几十年社会规范的放松意味着要禁止的事情越来越少。

sentences.en: The resulting wave of intolerance has been eerily similar in form to the Cultural Revolution, though fortunately much smaller in magnitude. [4]I've deliberately avoided mentioning any specific heresies here.
sentences.zh: 由此产生的不宽容浪潮在形式上与文化大革命惊人地相似，尽管幸运的是规模要小得多。 [4]我在这里故意避免提及任何具体的异端邪说。

sentences.en: Partly because one of the universal tactics of heretic hunters, now as in the past, is to accuse those who disapprove of the way in which they suppress ideas of being heretics themselves.
sentences.zh: 部分原因是，无论过去还是现在，异端猎手的普遍策略之一就是指责那些不同意他们压制异端思想的人。

sentences.en: Indeed, this tactic is so consistent that you could use it as a way of detecting witch hunts in any era.And that's the second reason I've avoided mentioning any specific heresies.
sentences.zh: 事实上，这种策略是如此一致，以至于你可以用它作为检测任何时代政治迫害的一种方式。这是我避免提及任何特定异端的第二个原因。

sentences.en: I want this essay to work in the future, not just now.
sentences.zh: 我希望这篇文章能够在未来发挥作用，而不仅仅是现在。

sentences.en: And unfortunately it probably will.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，它可能会。

sentences.en: The aggressively conventional-minded will always be among us, looking for things to forbid.
sentences.zh: 那些具有激进传统思想的人永远在我们中间，寻找着禁止的事物。

sentences.en: All they need is an ideology to tell them what.
sentences.zh: 他们所需要的只是一种意识形态来告诉他们什么。

sentences.en: And it's unlikely the current one will be the last.There are aggressively conventional-minded people on both the right and the left.
sentences.zh: 目前的情况不太可能是最后一次。左右两派都有激进的传统思想。

sentences.en: The reason the current wave of intolerance comes from the left is simply because the new unifying ideology happened to come from the left.
sentences.zh: 当前的不宽容浪潮之所以来自左派，只是因为新的统一意识形态恰好来自左派。

sentences.en: The next one might come from the right.
sentences.zh: 下一个可能来自右边。

sentences.en: Imagine what that would be like.Fortunately in western countries the suppression of heresies is nothing like as bad as it used to be.
sentences.zh: 想象一下那会是什么样子。幸运的是，在西方国家，对异端的镇压已经不像以前那么糟糕了。

sentences.en: Though the window of opinions you can express publicly has narrowed in the last decade, it's still much wider than it was a few hundred years ago.
sentences.zh: 尽管在过去十年中你可以公开表达意见的范围已经缩小，但仍然比几百年前宽得多。

sentences.en: The problem is the derivative.
sentences.zh: 问题是导数。

sentences.en: Up till about 1985 the window had been growing ever wider.
sentences.zh: 直到 1985 年左右，这个窗口变得越来越宽。

sentences.en: Anyone looking into the future in 1985 would have expected freedom of expression to continue to increase.
sentences.zh: 任何展望 1985 年未来的人都会期望言论自由会继续增加。

sentences.en: Instead it has decreased. [5]The situation is similar to what's happened with infectious diseases like measles.
sentences.zh: 相反，它有所减少。 [5]这种情况与麻疹等传染病的情况类似。

sentences.en: Anyone looking into the future in 2010 would have expected the number of measles cases in the US to continue to decrease.
sentences.zh: 任何展望 2010 年未来的人都会预计美国的麻疹病例数量将继续减少。

sentences.en: Instead, thanks to anti-vaxxers, it has increased.
sentences.zh: 相反，由于反疫苗者，它有所增加。

sentences.en: The absolute number is still not that high.
sentences.zh: 绝对数量仍然没有那么高。

sentences.en: The problem is the derivative. [6]In both cases it's hard to know how much to worry.
sentences.zh: 问题是导数。 [6]在这两种情况下，很难知道该担心到什么程度。

sentences.en: Is it really dangerous to society as a whole if a handful of extremists refuse to get their kids vaccinated, or shout down speakers at universities?
sentences.zh: 如果少数极端分子拒绝让他们的孩子接种疫苗，或者在大学里大声喧哗，这对整个社会真的很危险吗？

sentences.en: The point to start worrying is presumably when their efforts start to spill over into everyone else's lives.
sentences.zh: 大概当他们的努力开始蔓延到其他人的生活时，就会开始担心。

sentences.en: And in both cases that does seem to be happening.So it's probably worth spending some amount of effort on pushing back to keep open the window of free expression.
sentences.zh: 在这两种情况下，这似乎都在发生。因此，花一些精力来阻止言论自由的窗口可能是值得的。

sentences.en: My hope is that this essay will help form social antibodies not just against current efforts to suppress ideas, but against the concept of heresy in general.
sentences.zh: 我希望这篇文章将有助于形成社会抗体，不仅反对当前压制思想的努力，而且反对一般异端概念。

sentences.en: That's the real prize.
sentences.zh: 这才是真正的奖品。

sentences.en: How do you disable the concept of heresy?
sentences.zh: 你如何消除异端的概念？

sentences.en: Since the Enlightenment, western societies have discovered many techniques for doing that, but there are surely more to be discovered.Overall I'm optimistic.
sentences.zh: 自启蒙运动以来，西方社会已经发现了许多实现这一目标的技术，但肯定还有更多技术有待发现。总的来说，我很乐观。

sentences.en: Though the trend in freedom of expression has been bad over the last decade, it's been good over the longer term.
sentences.zh: 尽管过去十年言论自由的趋势很糟糕，但从长远来看却是好的。

sentences.en: And there are signs that the current wave of intolerance is peaking.
sentences.zh: 有迹象表明，当前的不宽容浪潮正在达到顶峰。

sentences.en: Independent-minded people I talk to seem more confident than they did a few years ago.
sentences.zh: 与我交谈过的具有独立思想的人似乎比几年前更加自信。

sentences.en: On the other side, even some of the leaders are starting to wonder if things have gone too far.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，甚至一些领导人也开始怀疑事情是否走得太远了。

sentences.en: And popular culture among the young has already moved on.
sentences.zh: 年轻人中的流行文化已经继续发展。

sentences.en: All we have to do is keep pushing back, and the wave collapses.
sentences.zh: 我们所要做的就是不断地反击，浪潮就会崩溃。

sentences.en: And then we'll be net ahead, because as well as having defeated this wave, we'll also have developed new tactics for resisting the next one.Notes[1] Or more accurately, biographies of Newton, since Westfall wrote two: a long version called Never at Rest, and a shorter one called The Life of Isaac Newton.
sentences.zh: 然后我们就会取得领先，因为除了击败这一波浪潮之外，我们还将制定新的策略来抵抗下一波浪潮。注释[1]或者更准确地说，牛顿的传记，因为韦斯特弗尔写了两本：一个较长的版本，称为“永不休息”，一个较短的版本，称为“艾萨克·牛顿的一生”。

sentences.en: Both are great.
sentences.zh: 两者都很棒。

sentences.en: The short version moves faster, but the long one is full of interesting and often very funny details.
sentences.zh: 短版动作更快，但长版充满了有趣且常常非常有趣的细节。

sentences.en: This passage is the same in both.[2] Another more subtle but equally damning bit of evidence is that claims of x-ism are never qualified.
sentences.zh: 这段话在两者中都是相同的。[2]另一个更微妙但同样具有毁灭性的证据是，X 主义的主张从来没有得到证实。

sentences.en: You never hear anyone say that a statement is "probably x-ist" or "almost certainly y-ist."
sentences.zh: 你永远不会听到有人说某个陈述“可能是 x 主义”或“几乎肯定是 y 主义”。

sentences.en: If claims of x-ism were actually claims about truth, you'd expect to see "probably" in front of "x-ist" as often as you see it in front of "fallacious."[3] The rules must be strict, but they need not be demanding.
sentences.zh: 如果x主义的主张实际上是关于真理的主张，那么你会期望在“x主义”前面看到“可能”，就像在“谬误”前面看到“可能”一样。 [3] 规则必须严格，但不必要求很高。

sentences.en: So the most effective type of rules are those about superficial matters, like doctrinal minutiae, or the precise words adherents must use.
sentences.zh: 因此，最有效的规则类型是那些关于表面问题的规则，例如教义细节，或者追随者必须使用的精确词语。

sentences.en: Such rules can be made extremely complicated, and yet don't repel potential converts by requiring significant sacrifice.The superficial demands of orthodoxy make it an inexpensive substitute for virtue.
sentences.zh: 这些规则可以变得极其复杂，但不会因需要重大牺牲而排斥潜在的皈依者。正统观念的肤浅要求使其成为美德的廉价替代品。

sentences.en: And that in turn is one of the reasons orthodoxy is so attractive to bad people.
sentences.zh: 这反过来也是正统观念对坏人如此有吸引力的原因之一。

sentences.en: You could be a horrible person, and yet as long as you're orthodox, you're better than everyone who isn't.[4] Arguably there were two.
sentences.zh: 你可能是一个可怕的人，但只要你是正统的，你就比所有不正统的人都要好。 [4]可以说有两个。

sentences.en: The first had died down somewhat by 2000, but was followed by a second in the 2010s, probably caused by social media.[5] Fortunately most of those trying to suppress ideas today still respect Enlightenment principles enough to pay lip service to them.
sentences.zh: 到 2000 年，第一次事件有所平息，但在 2010 年代又发生了第二次事件，可能是由社交媒体引起的。 [5]幸运的是，今天大多数试图压制思想的人仍然尊重启蒙运动的原则，足以在口头上支持它们。

sentences.en: They know they're not supposed to ban ideas per se, so they have to recast the ideas as causing "harm," which sounds like something that can be banned.
sentences.zh: 他们知道他们不应该禁止想法本身，因此他们必须将这些想法重新定义为造成“伤害”，这听起来像是可以被禁止的东西。

sentences.en: The more extreme try to claim speech itself is violence, or even that silence is.
sentences.zh: 更极端的尝试声称言论本身就是暴力，甚至沉默也是暴力。

sentences.en: But strange as it may sound, such gymnastics are a good sign.
sentences.zh: 尽管听起来很奇怪，但这样的体操是一个好兆头。

sentences.en: We'll know we're really in trouble when they stop bothering to invent pretenses for banning ideas — when, like the medieval church, they say "Damn right we're banning ideas, and in fact here's a list of them."[6] People only have the luxury of ignoring the medical consensus about vaccines because vaccines have worked so well.
sentences.zh: 当他们不再费心发明借口来禁止思想时，我们就会知道我们真的遇到了麻烦——就像中世纪的教会一样，他们说“该死的，我们正在禁止思想，事实上这里有一个列表。”[6]人们只能奢侈地忽视关于疫苗的医学共识，因为疫苗效果很好。

sentences.en: If we didn't have any vaccines at all, the mortality rate would be so high that most current anti-vaxxers would be begging for them.
sentences.zh: 如果我们根本没有任何疫苗，死亡率将会如此之高，以至于大多数当前的抗疫苗者都会乞求疫苗。

sentences.en: And the situation with freedom of expression is similar.
sentences.zh: 言论自由的情况也类似。

sentences.en: It's only because they live in a world created by the Enlightenment that kids from the suburbs can play at banning ideas.Thanks to Marc Andreessen, Chris Best, Trevor Blackwell, Nicholas Christakis, Daniel Gackle, Jonathan Haidt, Claire Lehmann, Jessica Livingston, Greg Lukianoff, Robert Morris, and Garry Tan for reading drafts of this.
sentences.zh: 只是因为他们生活在启蒙运动创造的世界中，来自郊区的孩子才可以玩禁止想法的游戏。感谢 Marc Andreessen、Chris Best、Trevor Blackwell、Nicholas Christakis、Daniel Gackle、Jonathan Haidt、Claire Lehmann、Jessica Livingston、Greg Lukianoff、Robert Morris 和 Garry Tan 阅读本文的草稿。

blocks.html_en: One of the most surprising things I've witnessed in my lifetime is the rebirth of the concept of heresy.

sentences.en: One of the most surprising things I've witnessed in my lifetime is the rebirth of the concept of heresy.
sentences.zh: 我一生中目睹的最令人惊讶的事情之一就是异端概念的重生。

blocks.html_en: In his excellent biography of Newton, Richard Westfall writes about the moment when he was elected a fellow of Trinity College:

sentences.en: In his excellent biography of Newton, Richard Westfall writes about the moment when he was elected a fellow of Trinity College:
sentences.zh: 理查德·韦斯特弗尔（Richard Westfall）在他出色的牛顿传记中描述了他被选为三一学院院士的那一刻：

blocks.html_en: Supported comfortably, Newton was free to devote himself wholly to whatever he chose. To remain on, he had only to avoid the three unforgivable sins: crime, heresy, and marriage. [1]

sentences.en: Supported comfortably, Newton was free to devote himself wholly to whatever he chose.
sentences.zh: 在舒适的支持下，牛顿可以自由地全身心投入到他选择的任何事情中。

sentences.en: To remain on, he had only to avoid the three unforgivable sins: crime, heresy, and marriage. [1]
sentences.zh: 为了继续留下来，他只需要避免三宗不可饶恕的罪孽：犯罪、异端和婚姻。 [1]

blocks.html_en: There are an ever-increasing number of opinions you can be fired for. Those doing the firing don't use the word "heresy" to describe them, but structurally they're equivalent. Structurally there are two distinctive things about heresy: (1) that it takes priority over the question of truth or falsity, and (2) that it outweighs everything else the speaker has done.

sentences.en: There are an ever-increasing number of opinions you can be fired for.
sentences.zh: 您可能会因为越来越多的意见而被解雇。

sentences.en: Those doing the firing don't use the word "heresy" to describe them, but structurally they're equivalent.
sentences.zh: 那些进行解雇的人并不使用“异端”这个词来描述他们，但在结构上他们是等效的。

sentences.en: Structurally there are two distinctive things about heresy: (1) that it takes priority over the question of truth or falsity, and (2) that it outweighs everything else the speaker has done.
sentences.zh: 从结构上讲，异端有两个独特之处：（1）它优先于真假问题，（2）它比演讲者所做的一切都重要。

blocks.html_en: For example, when someone calls a statement "x-ist," they're also implicitly saying that this is the end of the discussion. They do not, having said this, go on to consider whether the statement is true or not. Using such labels is the conversational equivalent of signalling an exception. That's one of the reasons they're used: to end a discussion.

sentences.en: For example, when someone calls a statement "x-ist," they're also implicitly saying that this is the end of the discussion.
sentences.zh: 例如，当有人将某个陈述称为“x-ist”时，他们也含蓄地表示讨论到此结束。

sentences.en: They do not, having said this, go on to consider whether the statement is true or not.
sentences.zh: 说了这句话后，他们并没有继续考虑这个说法是否属实。

sentences.en: Using such labels is the conversational equivalent of signalling an exception.
sentences.zh: 使用此类标签相当于会话中发出异常信号。

sentences.en: That's one of the reasons they're used: to end a discussion.
sentences.zh: 这就是使用它们的原因之一：结束讨论。

blocks.html_en: If you find yourself talking to someone who uses these labels a lot, it might be worthwhile to ask them explicitly if they believe any babies are being thrown out with the bathwater. Can a statement be x-ist, for whatever value of x, and also true? If the answer is yes, then they're admitting to banning the truth. That's obvious enough that I'd guess most would answer no. But if they answer no, it's easy to show that they're mistaken, and that in practice such labels are applied to statements regardless of their truth or falsity.

sentences.en: If you find yourself talking to someone who uses these labels a lot, it might be worthwhile to ask them explicitly if they believe any babies are being thrown out with the bathwater.
sentences.zh: 如果您发现自己与经常使用这些标签的人交谈，那么可能值得明确询问他们是否认为任何婴儿都被与洗澡水一起倒掉了。

sentences.en: Can a statement be x-ist, for whatever value of x, and also true?
sentences.zh: 无论 x 的值如何，一个陈述是否可以是 x-ist 并且为真？

sentences.en: If the answer is yes, then they're admitting to banning the truth.
sentences.zh: 如果答案是肯定的，那么他们就承认禁止真相。

sentences.en: That's obvious enough that I'd guess most would answer no.
sentences.zh: 这很明显，我猜大多数人都会回答“不”。

sentences.en: But if they answer no, it's easy to show that they're mistaken, and that in practice such labels are applied to statements regardless of their truth or falsity.
sentences.zh: 但如果他们的回答是否定的，那么很容易表明他们错了，而且在实践中，无论这些陈述是真还是假，这些标签都会被贴在陈述上。

blocks.html_en: The clearest evidence of this is that whether a statement is considered x-ist often depends on who said it. Truth doesn't work that way. The same statement can't be true when one person says it, but x-ist, and therefore false, when another person does.

sentences.en: The clearest evidence of this is that whether a statement is considered x-ist often depends on who said it.
sentences.zh: 最明显的证据是，一个陈述是否被视为 x-ist 通常取决于谁说的。

sentences.en: Truth doesn't work that way.
sentences.zh: 真相不是这样的。

sentences.en: The same statement can't be true when one person says it, but x-ist, and therefore false, when another person does.
sentences.zh: 同样的陈述当一个人说出来时不可能是真的，但当另一个人这么说时就不是了，因此是错误的。

blocks.html_en: The other distinctive thing about heresies, compared to ordinary opinions, is that the public expression of them outweighs everything else the speaker has done. In ordinary matters, like knowledge of history, or taste in music, you're judged by the average of your opinions. A heresy is qualitatively different. It's like dropping a chunk of uranium onto the scale.

sentences.en: The other distinctive thing about heresies, compared to ordinary opinions, is that the public expression of them outweighs everything else the speaker has done.
sentences.zh: 与普通观点相比，异端邪说的另一个独特之处在于，异端邪说的公开表达超过了演讲者所做的一切。

sentences.en: In ordinary matters, like knowledge of history, or taste in music, you're judged by the average of your opinions.
sentences.zh: 在平常的事情上，比如历史知识或音乐品味，人们会根据你的平均意见来评价你。

sentences.en: A heresy is qualitatively different.
sentences.zh: 异端在本质上是不同的。

sentences.en: It's like dropping a chunk of uranium onto the scale.
sentences.zh: 这就像将一大块铀滴到秤上。

blocks.html_en: Back in the day (and still, in some places) the punishment for heresy was death. You could have led a life of exemplary goodness, but if you publicly doubted, say, the divinity of Christ, you were going to burn. Nowadays, in civilized countries, heretics only get fired in the metaphorical sense, by losing their jobs. But the structure of the situation is the same: the heresy outweighs everything else. You could have spent the last ten years saving children's lives, but if you express certain opinions, you're automatically fired.

sentences.en: Back in the day (and still, in some places) the punishment for heresy was death.
sentences.zh: 在过去（现在仍然在某些地方），对异端的惩罚是死刑。

sentences.en: You could have led a life of exemplary goodness, but if you publicly doubted, say, the divinity of Christ, you were going to burn.
sentences.zh: 你本来可以过着模范善良的生活，但如果你公开怀疑，比如说，基督的神性，你就会被烧死。

sentences.en: Nowadays, in civilized countries, heretics only get fired in the metaphorical sense, by losing their jobs.
sentences.zh: 如今，在文明国家，异教徒只会在隐喻意义上被解雇，即失去工作。

sentences.en: But the structure of the situation is the same: the heresy outweighs everything else.
sentences.zh: 但情况的结构是一样的：异端压倒一切。

sentences.en: You could have spent the last ten years saving children's lives, but if you express certain opinions, you're automatically fired.
sentences.zh: 你本可以在过去的十年里拯救孩子们的生命，但如果你表达某些观点，你就会自动被解雇。

blocks.html_en: It's much the same as if you committed a crime. No matter how virtuously you've lived, if you commit a crime, you must still suffer the penalty of the law. Having lived a previously blameless life might mitigate the punishment, but it doesn't affect whether you're guilty or not.

sentences.en: It's much the same as if you committed a crime.
sentences.zh: 这与您犯了罪非常相似。

sentences.en: No matter how virtuously you've lived, if you commit a crime, you must still suffer the penalty of the law.
sentences.zh: 无论你的生活多么有道德，如果你犯了罪，你仍然必须受到法律的惩罚。

sentences.en: Having lived a previously blameless life might mitigate the punishment, but it doesn't affect whether you're guilty or not.
sentences.zh: 以前过着无可指责的生活可能会减轻惩罚，但这并不影响你是否有罪。

blocks.html_en: A heresy is an opinion whose expression is treated like a crime — one that makes some people feel not merely that you're mistaken, but that you should be punished. Indeed, their desire to see you punished is often stronger than it would be if you'd committed an actual crime. There are many on the far left who believe strongly in the reintegration of felons (as I do myself), and yet seem to feel that anyone guilty of certain heresies should never work again.

sentences.en: A heresy is an opinion whose expression is treated like a crime — one that makes some people feel not merely that you're mistaken, but that you should be punished.
sentences.zh: 异端邪说是一种观点，其表达被视为犯罪——这种观点不仅让一些人觉得你错了，而且觉得你应该受到惩罚。

sentences.en: Indeed, their desire to see you punished is often stronger than it would be if you'd committed an actual crime.
sentences.zh: 事实上，他们希望看到你受到惩罚的愿望往往比你真正犯罪时更强烈。

sentences.en: There are many on the far left who believe strongly in the reintegration of felons (as I do myself), and yet seem to feel that anyone guilty of certain heresies should never work again.
sentences.zh: 有许多极左人士坚信重罪犯重新融入社会（就像我自己一样），但似乎认为任何犯有某些异端邪说的人都不应该再工作。

blocks.html_en: There are always some heresies — some opinions you'd be punished for expressing. But there are a lot more now than there were a few decades ago, and even those who are happy about this would have to agree that it's so.

sentences.en: There are always some heresies — some opinions you'd be punished for expressing.
sentences.zh: 总有一些异端邪说——有些观点你会因为表达而受到惩罚。

sentences.en: But there are a lot more now than there were a few decades ago, and even those who are happy about this would have to agree that it's so.
sentences.zh: 但现在的数量比几十年前要多得多，即使是那些对此感到高兴的人也不得不承认事实确实如此。

blocks.html_en: Why? Why has this antiquated-sounding religious concept come back in a secular form? And why now?

sentences.en: Why?
sentences.zh: 为什么？

sentences.en: Why has this antiquated-sounding religious concept come back in a secular form?
sentences.zh: 为什么这个听起来过时的宗教概念又以世俗的形式回归呢？

sentences.en: And why now?
sentences.zh: 为什么现在呢？

blocks.html_en: You need two ingredients for a wave of intolerance: intolerant people, and an ideology to guide them. The intolerant people are always there. They exist in every sufficiently large society. That's why waves of intolerance can arise so suddenly; all they need is something to set them off.

sentences.en: You need two ingredients for a wave of intolerance: intolerant people, and an ideology to guide them.
sentences.zh: 形成不宽容浪潮需要两个要素：不宽容的人以及引导他们的意识形态。

sentences.en: The intolerant people are always there.
sentences.zh: 不宽容的人总是存在的。

sentences.en: They exist in every sufficiently large society.
sentences.zh: 它们存在于每一个足够大的社会中。

sentences.en: That's why waves of intolerance can arise so suddenly; all they need is something to set them off.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么不宽容的浪潮会突然出现。他们所需要的只是一些东西来引爆他们。

blocks.html_en: I've already written an

sentences.en: I've already written an
sentences.zh: 我已经写了一个

blocks.html_en: Normally they're only locally visible. They're the grumpy, censorious people in a group — the ones who are always first to complain when something violates the current rules of propriety. But occasionally, like a vector field whose elements become aligned, a large number of aggressively conventional-minded people unite behind some ideology all at once. Then they become much more of a problem, because a mob dynamic takes over, where the enthusiasm of each participant is increased by the enthusiasm of the others.

sentences.en: Normally they're only locally visible.
sentences.zh: 通常它们仅在本地可见。

sentences.en: They're the grumpy, censorious people in a group — the ones who are always first to complain when something violates the current rules of propriety.
sentences.zh: 他们是群体中脾气暴躁、挑剔的人——当有事情违反现行礼仪规则时，他们总是第一个抱怨的人。

sentences.en: But occasionally, like a vector field whose elements become aligned, a large number of aggressively conventional-minded people unite behind some ideology all at once.
sentences.zh: 但偶尔，就像元素对齐的向量场一样，大量具有攻击性的传统思想的人会同时团结在某种意识形态的支持下。

sentences.en: Then they become much more of a problem, because a mob dynamic takes over, where the enthusiasm of each participant is increased by the enthusiasm of the others.
sentences.zh: 然后，它们就变得更加成问题，因为暴民动态占据主导地位，每个参与者的热情都会因其他参与者的热情而增加。

blocks.html_en: The most notorious 20th century case may have been the Cultural Revolution. Though initiated by Mao to undermine his rivals, the Cultural Revolution was otherwise mostly a grass-roots phenomenon. Mao said in essence: There are heretics among us. Seek them out and punish them. And that's all the aggressively conventional-minded ever need to hear. They went at it with the delight of dogs chasing squirrels.

sentences.en: The most notorious 20th century case may have been the Cultural Revolution.
sentences.zh: 20世纪最臭名昭著的案例可能是文化大革命。

sentences.en: Though initiated by Mao to undermine his rivals, the Cultural Revolution was otherwise mostly a grass-roots phenomenon.
sentences.zh: 尽管文化大革命是毛泽东发起的，目的是削弱他的对手，但除此之外，文化大革命主要是一种草根现象。

sentences.en: Mao said in essence: There are heretics among us.
sentences.zh: 毛泽东实质上说：我们中间有异端。

sentences.en: Seek them out and punish them.
sentences.zh: 找出他们并惩罚他们。

sentences.en: And that's all the aggressively conventional-minded ever need to hear.
sentences.zh: 这就是那些思想激进、传统观念强的人所需要听到的。

sentences.en: They went at it with the delight of dogs chasing squirrels.
sentences.zh: 他们像狗追松鼠一样高兴。

blocks.html_en: To unite the conventional-minded, an ideology must have many of the features of a religion. In particular it must have strict and arbitrary rules that adherents can demonstrate their

sentences.en: To unite the conventional-minded, an ideology must have many of the features of a religion.
sentences.zh: 为了团结传统思想，意识形态必须具有宗教的许多特征。

sentences.en: In particular it must have strict and arbitrary rules that adherents can demonstrate their
sentences.zh: 特别是它必须有严格和任意的规则，让追随者能够证明他们的

blocks.html_en: In the late 1980s a new ideology of this type appeared in US universities. It had a very strong component of moral purity, and the aggressively conventional-minded seized upon it with their usual eagerness — all the more because the relaxation of social norms in the preceding decades meant there had been less and less to forbid. The resulting wave of intolerance has been eerily similar in form to the Cultural Revolution, though fortunately much smaller in magnitude.

sentences.en: In the late 1980s a new ideology of this type appeared in US universities.
sentences.zh: 20 世纪 80 年代末，美国大学中出现了这种类型的新意识形态。

sentences.en: It had a very strong component of moral purity, and the aggressively conventional-minded seized upon it with their usual eagerness — all the more because the relaxation of social norms in the preceding decades meant there had been less and less to forbid.
sentences.zh: 它具有很强的道德纯洁性成分，而那些具有激进传统思想的人以他们一贯的渴望抓住了它——更重要的是，因为过去几十年社会规范的放松意味着要禁止的事情越来越少。

sentences.en: The resulting wave of intolerance has been eerily similar in form to the Cultural Revolution, though fortunately much smaller in magnitude.
sentences.zh: 由此产生的不宽容浪潮在形式上与文化大革命惊人地相似，尽管幸运的是规模要小得多。

blocks.html_en: I've deliberately avoided mentioning any specific heresies here. Partly because one of the universal tactics of heretic hunters, now as in the past, is to accuse those who disapprove of the way in which they suppress ideas of being heretics themselves. Indeed, this tactic is so consistent that you could use it as a way of detecting witch hunts in any era.

sentences.en: I've deliberately avoided mentioning any specific heresies here.
sentences.zh: 我在这里故意避免提及任何具体的异端邪说。

sentences.en: Partly because one of the universal tactics of heretic hunters, now as in the past, is to accuse those who disapprove of the way in which they suppress ideas of being heretics themselves.
sentences.zh: 部分原因是，无论过去还是现在，异端猎手的普遍策略之一就是指责那些不同意他们压制异端思想的人。

sentences.en: Indeed, this tactic is so consistent that you could use it as a way of detecting witch hunts in any era.
sentences.zh: 事实上，这种策略是如此一致，以至于你可以用它作为检测任何时代政治迫害的一种方式。

blocks.html_en: And that's the second reason I've avoided mentioning any specific heresies. I want this essay to work in the future, not just now. And unfortunately it probably will. The aggressively conventional-minded will always be among us, looking for things to forbid. All they need is an ideology to tell them what. And it's unlikely the current one will be the last.

sentences.en: And that's the second reason I've avoided mentioning any specific heresies.
sentences.zh: 这是我避免提及任何具体异端邪说的第二个原因。

sentences.en: I want this essay to work in the future, not just now.
sentences.zh: 我希望这篇文章能够在未来发挥作用，而不仅仅是现在。

sentences.en: And unfortunately it probably will.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，它可能会。

sentences.en: The aggressively conventional-minded will always be among us, looking for things to forbid.
sentences.zh: 那些具有激进传统思想的人永远在我们中间，寻找着禁止的事物。

sentences.en: All they need is an ideology to tell them what.
sentences.zh: 他们所需要的只是一种意识形态来告诉他们什么。

sentences.en: And it's unlikely the current one will be the last.
sentences.zh: 目前的情况不太可能是最后一次。

blocks.html_en: There are aggressively conventional-minded people on both the right and the left. The reason the current wave of intolerance comes from the left is simply because the new unifying ideology happened to come from the left. The next one might come from the right. Imagine what that would be like.

sentences.en: There are aggressively conventional-minded people on both the right and the left.
sentences.zh: 左翼和右翼都有激进的传统思想。

sentences.en: The reason the current wave of intolerance comes from the left is simply because the new unifying ideology happened to come from the left.
sentences.zh: 当前的不宽容浪潮之所以来自左派，只是因为新的统一意识形态恰好来自左派。

sentences.en: The next one might come from the right.
sentences.zh: 下一个可能来自右边。

sentences.en: Imagine what that would be like.
sentences.zh: 想象一下那会是什么样子。

blocks.html_en: Fortunately in western countries the suppression of heresies is nothing like as bad as it used to be. Though the window of opinions you can express publicly has narrowed in the last decade, it's still much wider than it was a few hundred years ago. The problem is the derivative. Up till about 1985 the window had been growing ever wider. Anyone looking into the future in 1985 would have expected freedom of expression to continue to increase. Instead it has decreased.

content_html_zh: 四月 2022我一生中目睹的最令人惊讶的事情之一就是异端概念的重生。理查德·韦斯特弗尔（Richard Westfall）在他出色的牛顿传记中写道，他被选为三一学院院士的那一刻：在舒适的支持下，牛顿可以自由地完全奉献自己所选择的任何事情。 为了继续留下来，他只需要避免三宗不可饶恕的罪孽：犯罪、异端和婚姻。 [1] 我第一次读到它是在 20 世纪 90 年代，它听起来很有趣的中世纪。 多么奇怪啊，必须避免犯异端邪说。 但 20 年后我重读它时，它听起来像是对当代就业的描述。有越来越多的观点可以让你被解雇。 那些进行解雇的人并不使用“异端”这个词来描述他们，但在结构上他们是等效的。 从结构上讲，异端有两个独特之处：（1）它优先于真假问题，（2）它比演讲者所做的其他一切都重要。例如，当有人称某个陈述为“x-ist”时，他们也含蓄地表示讨论到此结束。 说了这句话后，他们并没有继续考虑这个说法是否属实。 使用此类标签相当于会话中发出异常信号。 这就是使用它们的原因之一：结束讨论。如果您发现自己正在与经常使用这些标签的人交谈，那么可能值得明确询问他们是否认为任何婴儿都被与洗澡水一起倒掉了。 无论 x 的值如何，一个陈述是否可以是 x-ist 并且为真？ 如果答案是肯定的，那么他们就承认禁止真相。 这很明显，我猜大多数人都会回答“不”。 但如果他们的回答是否定的，那么很容易表明他们是错误的，而且在实践中，这样的标签会被应用到声明上，无论其真实性或虚假性。最明显的证据是，一个声明是否被认为是x-ist通常取决于谁说它。 真相不是这样的。 同样的陈述当一个人说出来时不可能是真的，但当另一个人这么说时就不是了，因此是错误的。 [2]与普通观点相比，异端邪说的另一个独特之处在于，异端邪说的公开表达超过了演讲者所做的一切。 在平常的事情上，比如历史知识或音乐品味，人们会根据你的平均意见来评价你。 异端在本质上是不同的。 这就像把一大块铀扔到天平上。在过去（现在仍然在某些地方），对异端的惩罚是死刑。 你本来可以过着模范善良的生活，但如果你公开怀疑，比如说，基督的神性，你就会被烧死。 如今，在文明国家，异教徒只会在隐喻意义上被解雇，即失去工作。 但情况的结构是一样的：异端压倒一切。 你本可以在过去的十年里拯救孩子们的生命，但如果你表达某些观点，你就会自动被解雇。这就像你犯了罪一样。 无论你的生活多么有道德，如果你犯了罪，你仍然必须受到法律的惩罚。 过着无可指责的生活可能会减轻惩罚，但这并不影响你是否有罪。异端邪说是一种观点，其表达被视为犯罪——这种观点让一些人不仅觉得你错了，而且觉得你应该受到惩罚。 事实上，他们希望看到你受到惩罚的愿望往往比你真正犯罪时更强烈。 有许多极左人士坚信重罪犯重新融入社会（就像我自己一样），但似乎觉得任何犯有某些异端邪说的人都不应该再工作。总有一些异端邪说——有些观点你会因为表达而受到惩罚。 但现在比几十年前多了很多，即使是那些对此感到高兴的人也不得不同意事实如此。为什么呢？ 为什么这个听起来过时的宗教概念又以世俗的形式回归呢？ 为什么是现在？引发不宽容浪潮需要两个要素：不宽容的人以及引导他们的意识形态。 不宽容的人总是存在的。 它们存在于每一个足够大的社会中。 这就是为什么不宽容的浪潮会突然出现。他们所需要的只是一些东西来引爆他们。我已经写过一篇文章来描述激进的传统思想。 简而言之，人们可以根据（1）他们的独立性或传统思想程度，以及（2）他们对此的激进程度进行两个维度的分类。 思想激进、传统观念强的人是正统观念的执行者。通常他们只在当地可见。 他们是群体中脾气暴躁、挑剔的人——当有事情违反现行礼仪规则时，他们总是第一个抱怨的人。 但偶尔，就像元素对齐的向量场一样，大量具有攻击性的传统思想的人会同时团结在某种意识形态的支持下。 然后它们就成为一个更大的问题，因为暴民动态占据了主导地位，每个参与者的热情都因其他参与者的热情而增加。20世纪最臭名昭著的案例可能是文化大革命。 尽管文化大革命是毛泽东发起的，目的是削弱他的对手，但除此之外，文化大革命主要是一种草根现象。 毛泽东实质上说：我们中间有异端。 找出他们并惩罚他们。 这就是那些思想激进、传统观念强的人所需要听到的。 他们像狗追松鼠一样高兴。 为了团结传统思想，意识形态必须具有宗教的许多特征。 特别是，它必须有严格和任意的规则，追随者可以通过遵守来证明自己的纯洁性，并且它的追随者必须相信，任何遵守这些规则的人在道德上事实上都优于任何不遵守这些规则的人。 [3]20世纪80年代末，美国大学中出现了这种类型的新意识形态。 它具有很强的道德纯洁性成分，而那些具有激进传统思想的人以他们一贯的渴望抓住了它——更重要的是，因为过去几十年社会规范的放松意味着要禁止的事情越来越少。 由此产生的不宽容浪潮在形式上与文化大革命惊人地相似，尽管幸运的是规模要小得多。 [4]我在这里故意避免提及任何具体的异端邪说。 部分原因是，无论过去还是现在，异端猎手的普遍策略之一就是指责那些不同意他们压制异端思想的人。 事实上，这种策略是如此一致，以至于你可以用它作为检测任何时代政治迫害的一种方式。这是我避免提及任何特定异端的第二个原因。 我希望这篇文章能够在未来发挥作用，而不仅仅是现在。 不幸的是，它可能会。 那些具有激进传统思想的人永远在我们中间，寻找着禁止的事物。 他们所需要的只是一种意识形态来告诉他们什么。 目前的情况不太可能是最后一次。左右两派都有激进的传统思想。 当前的不宽容浪潮之所以来自左派，只是因为新的统一意识形态恰好来自左派。 下一个可能来自右边。 想象一下那会是什么样子。幸运的是，在西方国家，对异端的镇压已经不像以前那么糟糕了。 尽管在过去十年中你可以公开表达意见的范围已经缩小，但仍然比几百年前宽得多。 问题是导数。 直到 1985 年左右，这个窗口变得越来越宽。 任何展望 1985 年未来的人都会期望言论自由会继续增加。 相反，它有所减少。 [5]这种情况与麻疹等传染病的情况类似。 任何展望 2010 年未来的人都会预计美国的麻疹病例数量将继续减少。 相反，由于反疫苗者，它有所增加。 绝对数量仍然没有那么高。 问题是导数。 [6]在这两种情况下，很难知道该担心到什么程度。 如果少数极端分子拒绝让他们的孩子接种疫苗，或者在大学里大声喧哗，这对整个社会真的很危险吗？ 大概当他们的努力开始蔓延到其他人的生活时，就会开始担心。 在这两种情况下，这似乎都在发生。因此，花一些精力来阻止言论自由的窗口可能是值得的。 我希望这篇文章将有助于形成社会抗体，不仅反对当前压制思想的努力，而且反对一般异端概念。 这才是真正的奖品。 你如何消除异端的概念？ 自启蒙运动以来，西方社会已经发现了许多实现这一目标的技术，但肯定还有更多技术有待发现。总的来说，我很乐观。 尽管过去十年言论自由的趋势很糟糕，但从长远来看却是好的。 有迹象表明，当前的不宽容浪潮正在达到顶峰。 与我交谈过的具有独立思想的人似乎比几年前更加自信。 另一方面，甚至一些领导人也开始怀疑事情是否走得太远了。 年轻人中的流行文化已经继续发展。 我们所要做的就是不断地反击，浪潮就会崩溃。 然后我们就会取得领先，因为除了击败这一波浪潮之外，我们还将制定新的策略来抵抗下一波浪潮。注释[1]或者更准确地说，牛顿的传记，因为韦斯特弗尔写了两本：一个较长的版本，称为“永不休息”，一个较短的版本，称为“艾萨克·牛顿的一生”。 两者都很棒。 短版动作更快，但长版充满了有趣且常常非常有趣的细节。 这段话在两者中都是相同的。[2]另一个更微妙但同样具有毁灭性的证据是，X 主义的主张从来没有得到证实。 你永远不会听到有人说某个陈述“可能是 x 主义”或“几乎肯定是 y 主义”。 如果x主义的主张实际上是关于真理的主张，那么你会期望在“x主义”前面看到“可能”，就像在“谬误”前面看到“可能”一样。 [3] 规则必须严格，但不必要求很高。 因此，最有效的规则类型是那些关于表面问题的规则，例如教义细节，或者追随者必须使用的精确词语。 这些规则可以变得极其复杂，但不会因需要重大牺牲而排斥潜在的皈依者。正统观念的肤浅要求使其成为美德的廉价替代品。 这反过来也是正统观念对坏人如此有吸引力的原因之一。 你可能是一个可怕的人，但只要你是正统的，你就比所有不正统的人都要好。 [4]可以说有两个。 到 2000 年，第一次事件有所平息，但在 2010 年代又发生了第二次事件，可能是由社交媒体引起的。 [5]幸运的是，今天大多数试图压制思想的人仍然尊重启蒙运动的原则，足以在口头上支持它们。 他们知道他们不应该禁止想法本身，因此他们必须将这些想法重新定义为造成“伤害”，这听起来像是可以被禁止的东西。 更极端的尝试声称言论本身就是暴力，甚至沉默也是暴力。 尽管听起来很奇怪，但这样的体操是一个好兆头。 当他们不再费心发明借口来禁止思想时，我们就会知道我们真的遇到了麻烦——就像中世纪的教会一样，他们说“该死的，我们正在禁止思想，事实上这里有一个列表。”[6]人们只能奢侈地忽视关于疫苗的医学共识，因为疫苗效果很好。 如果我们根本没有任何疫苗，死亡率将会如此之高，以至于大多数当前的抗疫苗者都会乞求疫苗。 言论自由的情况也类似。 只是因为他们生活在启蒙运动创造的世界中，来自郊区的孩子才可以玩禁止想法的游戏。感谢 Marc Andreessen、Chris Best、Trevor Blackwell、Nicholas Christakis、Daniel Gackle、Jonathan Haidt、Claire Lehmann、Jessica Livingston、Greg Lukianoff、Robert Morris 和 Garry Tan 阅读本文的草稿。
